query: mechanism of action of omaveloxolone and its effect on NF-kB signaling

retrieved_docs.title: Triamcinolone -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Triamcinolone is a glucocorticoid in the corticosteroid drug class. It exhibits anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressant activity via inhibiting the phospholipase A2 enzyme on the cell membrane phospholipid layer, thereby hindering the breakdown of leukocyte lysosomal membranes and preventing the formation of arachidonic acid. [7] It decreases the expression of cyclooxygenase (COX) and lipoxygenase (LOX) and thus prevents the biosynthesis of prostaglandins and leukotrienes, respectively. Corticosteroids manifest anti-inflammatory effects via inhibiting macrophage and leukocyte migration to the affected site by reversing vascular dilation and permeability. These actions lead to reduced edema, erythema, and pruritus. An important anti-inflammatory mechanism is mediated by the inhibition of nuclear factor kappa-B (NF-κB), which leads to decreased protein expression of interleukin-6 (IL-6), interleukin-8 (IL-8), monocyte chemoattractant protein-1 (MCP-1), and COX-2. [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Rhabdomyolysis -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: Gene therapy is being explored for treating inherited muscular dystrophies such as Duchenne and Becker. This includes various technologies such as read-through of early stop codons and exon skipping. Edasalonexent (CAT-1004), a nuclear factor κB (NF-κB) inhibitor that combines structural elements of salicylic acid and docosahexaenoic acid, has been evaluated in a phase 3, placebo-controlled clinical trial with some reported success. ( NCT03703882 ). Vamorolone is a glucocorticoid that stabilizes membranes by reducing lipid peroxidation and inhibiting NF-kB-mediated inflammation; it has also demonstrated functional improvement of symptoms. Many other novel treatments are being studied. [20] [64]

retrieved_docs.title: Ibuprofen -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The potential involvement of NSAIDs in carcinogenesis has been studied for the other 2 enzymatic pathways, but the information is still limited. LOX isoforms, particularly 5-LOX, 12-LOX, and 15-LOX, have been discussed as potential contributors to tumor development and growth. 5-LOX is normally expressed only in immune cells and has been implicated in the early stages of colon cancer, carcinogenesis in oral cavity tissue, and the expression of chronic myeloid leukemia. [33] [34] [35] 12-LOX has proangiogenic functions, as it controls G1/S-phase arrest via regulation of Nf-kB and inhibition of Akt and mitogen-activated protein kinases. [4] 15-LOX isoforms promote cell senescence and suppress cell cycle progression. [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Sulfasalazine -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The pro-inflammatory NF-kB signaling pathway inhibits the phosphorylation and activation of adenosine monophosphate-activated protein kinase by 5-aminosalicylic acid. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Dexrazoxane -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Dexrazoxane was also found to enhance the viability of cells and impose cardiac function protection by attenuating the apoptosis of cardiomyocytes and necroptosis simultaneously after treatment with doxorubicin via interference of the p38MAPK/NF-κB pathways. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Prednisone -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: After cell surface receptor attachment and entry into the cell, prednisone enters the nucleus, binds, and activates specific nuclear receptors, resulting in altered gene expression and inhibiting pro-inflammatory cytokine production. This agent decreases the number of circulating lymphocytes, inducing cell differentiation, and stimulates apoptosis in sensitive tumor cell populations. The effects of glucocorticoids are subject to mediation by mechanisms that alter DNA replication within the nucleus. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Bazedoxifene -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The biomolecular antagonistic effects arise from a pro-apoptotic signal, enabling bazedoxifene to inhibit STAT3 and MAPK signaling pathways and effectively eliminate cancer cells. [12] This drug has an oral bioavailability of around 6%, exhibiting a linear increase in serum concentration in response to ascending dosage levels. Although the molecular mechanism by which an estrogen agonist such as bazedoxifene modulates bone regulation and remodeling is not fully elucidated, current hypotheses suggest reductions in osteoclastogenesis through mitochondrial and ER-mediated mechanisms, coupled with underlying effects on regulating serum calcium levels. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Triamcinolone -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Distribution: The duration of triamcinolone varies because it depends on the route of administration. IV administration has been shown to have a volume of distribution of 103L. Oral bioavailability was 23%. [10] Metabolism: Systemically absorbed triamcinolone is hepatically metabolized via the CYP450 enzyme system; it is a CYP3A4 substrate. Elimination: Elimination is via both renal and fecal pathways.

retrieved_docs.title: Topical Corticosteroids -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The anti-inflammatory effects of topical corticosteroids include vasoconstriction, inhibition of phospholipase A2 release, and a direct inhibitory action on DNA and inflammatory transcription factors. [14] [15] Vasoconstriction of the blood vessels within the upper dermis decreases the number of inflammatory mediators delivered to the region. [14] The anti-inflammatory effect is also attributed to the synthesis of lipocortin, which inhibits phospholipase A2, thereby reducing the production of prostaglandins and leukotrienes. [15] Topical corticosteroids also act directly at the DNA level to increase the expression of anti-inflammatory genes and indirectly inhibit inflammatory transcription factors, such as NF-kB, to decrease the expression of pro-inflammatory genes. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Romosozumab -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Sclerostin is a secreted, osteocyte-derived glycoprotein that binds to low-density lipoprotein receptor proteins 5 and 6 (LRP5 and LRP6), preventing the activation of the Wnt/β-catenin pathway that normally stimulates osteoblasts. [17] Preventing this activation promotes the ubiquitination and degradation of β-catenin, which blocks nuclear import and inhibits the osteogenic function of osteoblasts, resulting in decreased bone formation. Additionally, sclerostin stimulates the production of RANKL (receptor activator of nuclear factor-κB ligand) and suppresses the production of OPG (osteoprotegerin) in osteoblasts and pre-osteoblasts. Increased RANKL and reduced OPG activates and induces differentiation of osteoclasts.

retrieved_docs.title: Imiquimod -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Imiquimod is an immune response modifier of the imidazoquinolinamines drug class that induces the production of various cytokines, such as Interferon-alpha, Interferon-gamma, tumor necrosis factor-alpha, interleukin-1, interleukin-6, and interleukin-8. [10] [11] Imiquimod activates the innate and adaptive immune responses via binding to Toll-like receptor 7 and activation of nuclear factor kappa-B with the resultant cytokine release and recruitment of plasmacytoid predendritic cells to the site of medication application with subsequent release of Interferon-alpha and a Th1 response. [12] [13] [14] [15] In addition to its anti-viral activity, imiquimod induces apoptosis of skin cancer cells and has demonstrated anti-tumoral activity. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Sulfasalazine -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Salicylates and sulfasalazine inhibit leukocyte accumulation by an adenosine-dependent mechanism independent of inhibiting the NF-kB pathway or prostaglandin synthesis. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Triamcinolone -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify appropriate clinical conditions for triamcinolone therapy, considering indications such as dermatological disorders, allergies, and joint inflammation. Screen patients for contraindications, potential drug interactions, and existing medical conditions that may affect triamcinolone treatment. Select the optimal triamcinolone formulation and route of administration based on patient-specific factors and treatment goals. Develop clear communication with patients about triamcinolone therapy's risks, benefits, and expectations, including potential side effects and proper usage. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Adapalene -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: In addition to follicular hyperkeratinization, acne is also a disorder associated with inflammation. Propionibacterium acnes is a gram-positive, anaerobic rod found in the sebaceous follicle. P. acnes is present in both acne-prone individuals and those without acne. The hypothesis is that there may be differences in the strains of P. acnes or in the host response to P. acnes, which leads to its varying degrees of pathogenicity. P. acnes release mediators that contribute to comedones rupture and stimulate inflammatory cells. P. acnes stimulates the toll-like receptor II (TLR-2) pathway, which releases pro-inflammatory modulators. This action leads to neutrophil recruitment and the release of enzymes that result in follicular epithelium rupture. One mediator, IL-12, promotes a TH1 immune response. Adapalene is thought to suppress polymorphonuclear lymphocyte chemotaxis and down-regulate 15-lipoxygenase and TLR-2, contributing to its anti-inflammatory effects. The anti-inflammatory action of adapalene is comparable to betamethasone-17-valerate and indomethacin. [9] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Sulfasalazine -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Sulfasalazine inhibits transcription factor nuclear factor kappa-B (NF-kB), thereby suppressing the transcription of NF-kB responsive pro-inflammatory genes, including TNF-α. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2467
retrieved_docs.content: Triamcinolone, identified as “triamcinolon,” is classified within the group of moderately potent corticosteroids that are used in various combination formulations (“Corticosteroids, moderately potent, other combinations”). As a member of this corticosteroid class, triamcinolone possesses anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive properties that make it a therapeutic option for managing multiple sclerosis (التصلب اللويحي). By recognizing triamcinolone’s place among moderately potent corticosteroids, clinicians can better understand its mechanism of action, appropriate dosing, and potential side‑effect profile when it is employed to reduce inflammation and disease activity in patients with multiple sclerosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1817
retrieved_docs.content: Prednisone, rimexolone, and paramethasone are all members of the class of systemic glucocorticoids. Systemic glucocorticoids constitute a group of steroid medications that are administered throughout the body to exert anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects. By being part of this class, each of these agents shares the common pharmacologic properties of glucocorticoids, including modulation of gene expression, reduction of cytokine production, and suppression of immune cell activity, which are leveraged in the treatment of a wide range of inflammatory and autoimmune conditions. Understanding that prednisone, rimexolone, and paramethasone belong to the systemic glucocorticoid category helps clinicians select appropriate therapy, anticipate similar side‑effect profiles, and apply consistent monitoring strategies across these related drugs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1971
retrieved_docs.content: Glucocorticoids, Systemic constitute a class of potent anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive agents, and the drugs prednison, meprednisone and paramethason are members of this systemic glucocorticoid group. As systemic glucocorticoids, these medications share mechanisms of action that involve binding to intracellular glucocorticoid receptors, modulating gene transcription, and reducing the production of pro‑inflammatory cytokines. Consequently, they are employed in the management of a wide range of inflammatory, autoimmune, and allergic conditions, as well as in certain oncologic and endocrine disorders. Understanding that prednison, meprednisone and paramethason belong to the Glucocorticoids, Systemic class helps clinicians anticipate their therapeutic effects, potential side‑effect profiles, and appropriate dosing strategies across diverse patient populations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 4 -- Community 2906
retrieved_docs.content: Antiflogistika, a class of anti‑inflammatory agents, includes several corticosteroid medications that are commonly used to control inflammatory conditions. The drugs classified as antiflogistika are clobetasol, diflorasone, flurandrenolone, prednicarbate, and triamcinolone acetonide. Each of these agents exerts potent anti‑inflammatory effects through glucocorticoid receptor activation, making them valuable options in the management of dermatologic, allergic, and other inflammatory disorders. Recognizing that these specific corticosteroids belong to the broader antiflogistika category helps clinicians select appropriate anti‑inflammatory therapy and understand their shared mechanism of action.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1630
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic glucocorticoids constitute a pharmacologic class of corticosteroid agents that are administered throughout the body to exert anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects. Representative members of this class include deflazacort, rimexolone, and paramethasone (also referred to as paramethason). By sharing the core glucocorticoid structure, these drugs act on glucocorticoid receptors to modulate gene transcription, reduce cytokine production, and suppress immune cell activation, making them useful in the treatment of autoimmune diseases, allergic reactions, and various inflammatory disorders. Recognizing that deflazacort, rimexolone, and paramethasone are all members of the systemic glucocorticoid category helps clinicians select appropriate agents based on potency, pharmacokinetics, and side‑effect profiles, thereby optimizing patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1846
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic glucocorticoids constitute a class of corticosteroid drugs that exert broad anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive actions throughout the body. Both prednison and rimexolone are individual agents that belong to this systemic glucocorticoid class, sharing the common pharmacologic properties of glucocorticoid receptor activation, suppression of cytokine production, and modulation of immune cell function. Recognizing prednison and rimexolone as members of the systemic glucocorticoid group helps clinicians understand their therapeutic indications, dosing considerations, and potential side‑effect profiles, which are characteristic of systemic glucocorticoid therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2540
retrieved_docs.content: Betamethasone is identified as a member of the class of corticosteroid ophthalmologic and otologic preparations, indicating its use in eye and ear formulations. It also belongs to the broader group of corticosteroids acting locally, reflecting its application in topical or localized therapy. As an anti‑inflammatory steroid, betamethasone functions to reduce inflammation in the tissues where it is applied. In addition to its pharmacologic classification, the Arabic term الصَّدَفِيَّة is noted as a possible treatment associated with betamethasone, suggesting that this agent may be managed or counteracted by that therapy. No contradictions arise among these classifications, and together they provide a comprehensive view of betamethasone’s therapeutic category, mechanism of action, and potential adjunctive management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1849
retrieved_docs.content: Betamethason is an anti‑inflammatory steroid that serves as the active ingredient of the cutaneous dose product “Betamethasone and clioquinol only product in cutaneous dose form.” Clinical guidelines such as the ABCDV protocol and the EACB protocol are mapped to betamethason, reflecting its role within these treatment frameworks. In addition, betamethason is linked to the formulation or brand name oterna. Therapeutic management of conditions involving betamethason may include the approach referred to as الصَّدَفِيَّة.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2197
retrieved_docs.content: Antiflogistika, the therapeutic class of anti‑inflammatory agents, encompasses several potent corticosteroids including clobetasol, clocortolone, halcinonid, triamcinolonacetonid, and halobetasol. Each of these drugs is classified as an antiflogistika, reflecting their shared mechanism of reducing inflammation. Halobetasol, in particular, is identified as having the therapeutic class of antiflogistika, confirming its role within this anti‑inflammatory group. Together, these agents illustrate the composition of the antiflogistika class and its relevance for managing inflammatory conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1305
retrieved_docs.content: Beklomethason and flutikason are both members of the potent group III corticosteroids and are classified as antiflogistika, indicating that they function as anti‑inflammatory agents. As members of this high‑potency corticosteroid class, they share similar mechanisms of action and therapeutic applications in reducing inflammation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 427
retrieved_docs.content: Deflazacort is classified within the therapeutic category “Immunological Agents [TC]” and is a member of the broader drug class “Glucocorticoids, Systemic.” Within this systemic glucocorticoid class, deflazacort shares its pharmacologic grouping with other agents such as meprednisone and paramethason, all of which are systemic glucocorticoids used for immunologic and anti‑inflammatory purposes. Recognizing that deflazacort, meprednisone, and paramethason belong to the same systemic glucocorticoid class helps clinicians understand their similar mechanisms of action, therapeutic indications, and potential side‑effect profiles, thereby supporting more informed prescribing and patient‑care decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 4 -- Community 2908
retrieved_docs.content: Antiflogistika refers to a therapeutic class of potent anti‑inflammatory agents that includes the corticosteroids clobetasol, halobetasol, clocortolone and flurandrenolon. All four compounds share the same anti‑inflammatory mechanism of action and are classified under the antiflogistika group, which is used to treat various inflammatory and dermatologic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2553
retrieved_docs.content: Triamcinolone is a corticosteroid that belongs to the moderately potent (group II) class of corticosteroids and is formulated for local oral treatment. It is recognized as a therapeutic option that may be used in the management of multiple sclerosis (التصلب اللويحي). Alclometasone is likewise a member of the moderately potent (group II) corticosteroid group. Both triamcinolone and alclometasone share the same potency classification within the broader corticosteroid category, which guides clinicians in selecting appropriate anti‑inflammatory agents for various conditions, including the potential use of triamcinolone in multiple sclerosis therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 609
retrieved_docs.content: Deflazacort, prednison, rimexolone, and paramethason are all members of the therapeutic class identified as Glucocorticoids, Systemic, which falls under the broader category of Immunological Agents [TC]. As systemic glucocorticoids, each of these agents possesses anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive properties that are utilized in the treatment of various immune‑mediated and inflammatory conditions. Their classification within Immunological Agents [TC] highlights their role in modulating immune responses, and recognizing them as part of the same systemic glucocorticoid group aids clinicians in understanding their similar mechanisms of action, potential side‑effect profiles, and therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1840
retrieved_docs.content: Amcinonide, deflazacort, klobetasol, halobetasol, and clocortolone are all classified within the therapeutic class antiflogistika, which denotes anti‑inflammatory agents. Each of these corticosteroid medications shares the common property of providing anti‑inflammatory effects, and their inclusion in the antiflogistika class reflects their similar mechanism of action and clinical use for reducing inflammation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1599
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroid [EPC] is a broad pharmacologic class that includes the specific agents amcinonide, clocortolone, flurandrenolon, halobetasol, klobetasol and prednicarbate, each of which is considered a parent member within this category. Recognizing that these individual corticosteroids belong to the overarching Corticosteroid [EPC] group helps clinicians understand their shared mechanisms of action, therapeutic indications, and potential class‑related effects, thereby supporting more informed prescribing and patient‑care decisions.
